clinical.title: Medico-legal practice in anaesthesia: a guide on how to get started as an expert witness

steps.text: Understand the role of an expert witness in civil litigation, focusing on advising the court on whether a clinician's actions meet acceptable professional standards (the Bolam test) and can be supported on a logical basis (the Bolitho test).
Maintain strict impartiality and independence, preparing reports for the court rather than acting as a partisan advocate for either the claimant or the defendant.
Ensure you possess a comprehensive, up-to-date knowledge of what constituted acceptable clinical practice at the specific time of the events under scrutiny.
Develop the core analytical skills required to derive a logical sequence of events from large, often disorganized or incomplete clinical records.
Translate complex medical concepts and clinical events into clear, grammatical, and accessible language that an intelligent layman can easily comprehend.
Strictly avoid stepping outside your specific area of clinical expertise, regardless of any other perceived failings in patient care.
Adhere to strict legal deadlines and maintain professional composure when robustly challenged during conferences with counsel or in the witness box.
Ensure appropriate professional indemnity is in place and be aware of the risks of adverse judicial comments if duties are not fulfilled fairly and fully.

clinical.summaryText: This guideline provides a comprehensive introduction for clinical anaesthetists looking to establish a medico-legal practice as an expert witness. It outlines the key differences between personal injury and clinical negligence work, emphasizing the expert's primary duty to advise the court impartially on the standards of care (using the Bolam and Bolitho tests) and causation. The document highlights the essential skills required, such as analytical report writing, adhering to strict deadlines, and maintaining professional composure under cross-examination. It encourages diversity among experts, noting that experienced Specialty and Associate Specialist (SAS) doctors, alongside consultants, can successfully undertake this role provided they have appropriate indemnity and robust clinical experience.

clinical.title: SAS Handbook

steps.text: Recognize and integrate SAS and locally-employed doctors as a vital, rapidly growing cohort comprising at least 20% of the anaesthetic workforce.
Explore and navigate 'alternative career pathways' tailored to the professional development of SAS anaesthetists.
Establish clear frameworks for clinical supervision, autonomy, and structured pathways for seeking assistance during clinical duties.
Facilitate personal development planning (PDP) tailored to the specific career progression and educational needs of SAS anaesthetists.
Provide robust professional support systems to address isolation, promote wellbeing, and ensure dignity and respect in the workplace.
Support leadership and management development opportunities to empower SAS doctors within clinical directorates.
Manage financial matters and contractual considerations relevant to SAS anaesthetists.
Assist candidates preparing for the Fellowship of the Royal College of Anaesthetists (FRCA) examinations and navigating the portfolio pathway to Specialist registration.
Deliver targeted orientation, mentorship, and support for international medical graduates (IMGs) transitioning into NHS anaesthetic practice.
Implement and advocate for the 'SAS Six' principles to improve working conditions, recognition, and career development.

clinical.summaryText:
The Association of Anaesthetists (AAGBI) SAS Handbook (2025, 5th edition) serves as a comprehensive resource for Staff, Associate Specialist, and Specialty (SAS) anaesthetists, who make up over 20% of the anaesthetic workforce. It addresses critical professional areas including alternative career pathways, clinical supervision, autonomy, personal development planning, and leadership. Additionally, the handbook provides essential guidance on wellbeing, financial matters, the FRCA examination, and the portfolio pathway to Specialist registration.

Recognizing the unique challenges faced by international medical graduates (IMGs) entering the NHS, this edition introduces dedicated support resources. It also outlines the 'SAS Six' principles to promote dignity, respect, and career progression, aiming to reduce professional isolation and ensure SAS doctors receive the recognition and support they deserve within the clinical workforce.

clinical.title: Who is the anaesthetist? Your guide to the medical specialty

steps.text: Manage patient discomfort and pain during operations (analgesia).
Maintain physiological and biochemical stability while the patient is asleep or sedated.
Provide support for body systems when a patient is critically ill (critical care).
Manage acute and chronic pain in various settings, including maternity wards and emergency departments.
Provide immediate life support care during emergencies such as cardiac arrest or traumatic accidents.

clinical.summaryText:
Anaesthetists are highly trained medical specialists responsible for ensuring patient safety throughout the entire surgical and critical care journey. Their core function involves managing pain, maintaining physiological stability (including breathing and biochemistry), and supporting complex body systems while patients undergo procedures or remain critically ill.

The scope of practice extends far beyond the operating theatre; anaesthetists are integral members of hospital teams, providing care in emergency departments, maternity wards, trauma settings, and during cardiac arrest. The specialty requires deep knowledge across anatomy, physiology, pharmacology, and physics, combined with essential soft skills like empathy, communication, and calmness under pressure.

clinical.title: Pre-operative Assessment and Patient Preparation: The Role of the Anaesthetist

steps.text: Anaesthetists should assume a central role in the organisation of pre-operative services that encompass much more than preparing the delivery of anaesthesia. The anaesthetist has the skills necessary to assess, optimise and estimate risk and support patients deciding whether to proceed with surgery and anaesthesia.
Pre-operative anaesthetic assessment should minimise risk for all patients as well as identify patients at particularly high risk.
The pre-operative anaesthetic clinic should co-operate with primary care to achieve these aims.

Skilled nurse practitioners are safe and cost-effective in preparing patients for anaesthesia and should work closely with anaesthetists with a special interest in pre-operative assessment and preparation.
After scheduled admissions, anaesthetists must confirm that patients have been prepared adequately by pre-operative services so that anaesthesia and surgery can proceed safely.
Tests performed before surgery should be limited to those recommended by national and local guidelines and protocols.
Most anaesthetic departments should plan for one consultant whole-time equivalent to run and manage daily high-risk clinics with appropriate secretarial support.

clinical.summaryText: This guideline outlines the essential role of the anaesthetist in pre-operative assessment and patient preparation. It emphasizes that anaesthetists must take a central role in organizing pre-operative services, which includes assessing, optimizing, and estimating risk to support patient decision-making. The guideline advocates for collaborative multidisciplinary care involving skilled nurse practitioners and primary care, while ensuring pre-operative testing is rationalized according to national and local protocols.

clinical.title: Checking anaesthetic equipment

steps.text: The anaesthetist must understand the function of all anaesthetic equipment and perform a thorough check before use.
Ensure that the user is trained and competent to operate the specific equipment being used.
Confirm that a self-inflating bag is immediately available in the location where anaesthesia may be given.
Check the breathing system, vaporisers, and ventilator individually for correct functioning.
Perform a two-bag test after checking all components to ensure proper function of the entire circuit.
Maintain a detailed record documenting that these pre-use checks have been completed with the anaesthetic machine.
Pay particular attention to and document the 'first user' check following any servicing or maintenance of the equipment.

clinical.summaryText:
A pre-use check is essential for patient safety to ensure the correct functioning of all anaesthetic equipment. The primary responsibility lies with the anaesthetist, who must be competent and trained in the use of the machinery. Key checks include ensuring a self-inflating bag is immediately available, individually testing the breathing system, vaporisers, and ventilator, and performing a two-bag test to validate the entire circuit.

Furthermore, meticulous documentation is mandatory. A record must be kept confirming that these comprehensive pre-use checks have been performed with the machine. The 'first user' check following servicing is highlighted as being especially critical and must also be recorded.

clinical.title: Immediate post-anaesthesia recovery
clinical.summaryText: All patients recovering from general, epidural, or spinal anaesthesia must be managed in a specially designated Post-Anaesthesia Care Unit (PACU) that adheres to established standards. Key requirements include formal handover of care from the anaesthetist to an appropriately trained PACU practitioner and implementing written criteria for discharge to the ward. Monitoring is paramount: patients require continuous observation until stable, involving assessment of airway control, cardiovascular stability, respiratory function, and ability to communicate. The facility must maintain robust emergency call systems, adequate staffing levels (minimum two staff, including one registered practitioner), and comprehensive audit/incident reporting mechanisms.

steps.text: After general, epidural or spinal anaesthesia, all patients should be recovered in a specially designated area (henceforth ‘post-anaesthesia care unit’, PACU) that complies with the standards and recommendations described in this document.
The anaesthetist must formally hand over the care of a patient to an appropriately trained and registered PACU practitioner.
Agreed, written criteria for discharge of patients from the PACU to the ward should be in place in all units.
An effective emergency call system must be in place in every PACU and tested regularly.
No fewer than two staff (of whom at least one must be a registered practitioner) should be present when there is a patient in a PACU who does not fulfil the criteria for discharge to the ward.
All registered practitioners should be appropriately trained in accordance with the standards and competencies detailed in the UK National Core Competencies for Post Anaesthesia Care.

All patients must be observed on a one-to-one basis by an anaesthetist or registered PACU practitioner until they have regained control of their airway, have stable cardiovascular and respiratory systems and are awake and able to communicate.
All patients with tracheal tubes in place in a PACU should be monitored with continuous capnography. The removal of tracheal tubes is the responsibility of the anaesthetist.
There should be a specially designated area for the recovery of children that is appropriately equipped and staffed.

All standards and recommendations described in this document should be applied to all areas in which patients recover after anaesthesia, to include those anaesthetics given for obstetric, cardiology, imaging and dental procedures, and in psychiatric units and community hospitals. Only registered PACU practitioners who are familiar with these areas should be allocated to recover patients in them as and when required.
Patients’ dignity and privacy should be respected at all times but patients’ safety must always be the primary concern.

When critically ill patients are managed in a PACU because of bed shortages, the primary responsibility for the patient lies with the hospital’s critical care team. The standard of nursing and medical care should be equal to that in the hospital’s critical care units.
Audit and critical incident reporting systems should be in place in all PACUs.

clinical.title: Peri-operative management of the obese surgical patient

steps.text: Management must encompass both organizational and clinical aspects of peri-operative care for obese patients.
The guidelines aim to inform best practice management throughout the entire peri-operative period.
Care must be delivered by members of a multidisciplinary team.
Specific attention should be paid to new material covering pharmacology, optimal positioning, and management of sleep-disordered breathing.

clinical.summaryText: These guidelines provide comprehensive advice for the organizational and clinical peri-operative care of obese surgical patients. They acknowledge that obesity presents a distinct set of challenges compared to non-obese individuals, requiring specialized attention across all phases of surgery. The recommendations are based on expert consensus, incorporating modern knowledge regarding pharmacology, positioning techniques, and addressing sleep-disordered breathing within the context of multidisciplinary team care.

clinical.title: Anaesthetic practice in the independent sector

steps.text: Transition terminology from 'private practice' to 'independent practice' to accurately reflect diverse funding sources including NHS-funded care, private medical insurance, and patient self-pay.
Ensure strict compliance with the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) regulations, which govern financial and organizational aspects of independent healthcare.
Proactively address pre-operative assessment and patient optimisation, establishing the anaesthetist's role as a 'peri-operative physician' within independent hospitals.
Adapt clinical practice to the changing demographics of patients undergoing treatment in independent hospitals, ensuring high standards of safety and care.
Verify that appropriate medical indemnity and clinical governance structures are in place for all independent sector activities.

clinical.summaryText: The Association of Anaesthetists (AAGBI) 2018 guideline on Anaesthetic Practice in the Independent Sector provides comprehensive guidance for consultants working outside the traditional NHS framework. It highlights the rapid growth of NHS-funded patients within independent hospitals and the transition from 'private' to 'independent' practice to reflect diverse funding streams, including NHS, private medical insurance, and self-pay. Key focus areas include adapting to changing patient demographics, complying with Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) regulations, and establishing the anaesthetist's role as a peri-operative physician. The guideline emphasizes the importance of robust pre-operative assessment, clinical optimisation, and maintaining high standards of clinical governance and indemnity in the independent sector.

clinical.title: Safe practice of total intravenous anaesthesia (TIVA) 2018

steps.text: All anaesthetists should be trained and competent in the delivery of TIVA. Schools of Anaesthesia and training bodies should provide teaching, training and practical experience of TIVA to all anaesthetic and intensive care medicine trainees. Consultant and SAS anaesthetists have a responsibility to ensure they have the knowledge and skills required to deliver TIVA competently and safely.
When general anaesthesia is to be maintained by propofol infusion, use of a target-controlled infusion (TCI) is recommended.

Starting target concentrations should be chosen depending on the characteristics of the patient, co-administered drugs and clinical situation. Older, frail or unwell patients may benefit from setting a low initial target propofol concentration, and making repeated small incremental increases.
Within an anaesthetic department, it is preferable to stock only one concentration of propofol and to dilute remifentanil to a single, standard concentration.

The infusion set through which TIVA is delivered should have a Luer-lock connector at each end, an antisyphon valve on the drug delivery line(s) and an anti-reflux valve on any fluid administration line. Drug and fluid lines should join as close to the patient as possible to minimise dead space. The use of administration sets specifically designed for TIVA is recommended.
Infusion pumps should be programmed only after the syringe containing the drug to be infused has been placed in the pump.
The intravenous cannula or central venous catheter through which the infusion is being delivered should, whenever practical, be visible throughout anaesthesia.

Anaesthetists should be familiar with the principles, interpretation and limitations of processed electroencephalogram (pEEG) monitoring. Observation of the EEG trace and electromyography activity is likely to improve the clinical utility of the monitoring.
Use of a processed EEG (pEEG) monitor is recommended when a neuromuscular blocking drug is used with TIVA.
When TIVA is administered outside the operating room, the same standards of practice and monitoring should apply as for anaesthesia in the operating room.

clinical.summaryText:
These guidelines, jointly developed by the Association of Anaesthetists and the Society for Intravenous Anaesthesia (SIVA), outline safe practices for administering total intravenous anaesthesia (TIVA). Prompted by findings from the 5th National Audit Project (NAP5) which highlighted a higher incidence of accidental awareness during general anaesthesia with TIVA due to inadequate training, this document establishes standards to mitigate these risks. Key recommendations focus on ensuring adequate training, utilizing target-controlled infusions (TCI) for propofol maintenance, standardizing drug concentrations, and employing specific infusion set configurations (such as Luer-lock connectors, antisyphon, and anti-reflux valves) to prevent delivery failures.

Additionally, the guidelines emphasize the importance of maintaining visibility of the intravenous access site, utilizing processed electroencephalogram (pEEG) monitoring—especially when neuromuscular blocking drugs are co-administered—and maintaining identical standards of care and monitoring when TIVA is delivered outside the traditional operating room environment.

clinical.title: Checklist for draw-over anaesthetic equipment 2019

steps.text: Ensure the anaesthetic practitioner is fully trained and competent in the use of draw-over anaesthetic equipment before clinical use.
Verify that a self-inflating bag is immediately available in any location where anaesthesia is administered.
Perform a full equipment check at the start of every operating session, individually checking the breathing system, vaporisers, and ventilator.
Conduct breathing system leak tests using the two-bag test and perform a vaporiser leak test as essential components of the checklist.
Perform and record a 'first user' check after any equipment servicing has occurred.
Document verification of checklist completion and any local constraints (such as electricity or oxygen supply) as part of the WHO safety briefing.
Utilize the short checklist between cases and document its use as part of the WHO safety briefing.
Only anaesthetise children if appropriate equipment based on the child's size is available and the practitioner has completed training in paediatric circuits.
Maintain a physical or digital record with the anaesthetic machine confirming that all required checks have been carried out.

clinical.summaryText: This guideline provides a structured checklist and clinical recommendations to ensure the correct functioning of draw-over anaesthetic equipment, which is critical for patient safety in diverse environments including military, disaster relief, and low-resource settings. It emphasizes that anaesthetists must be fully trained and competent before using draw-over apparatus, and highlights the necessity of having a self-inflating bag immediately available. Key safety procedures include performing a full equipment check at the start of each session, conducting a two-bag test and vaporiser leak test, and documenting these checks as part of the WHO safety briefing.

clinical.title: Day-case surgery

steps.text: Thorough anaesthetist-led, nurse-delivered pre-anaesthetic assessment and preparation, as well as protocol-driven discharge, are fundamental to safe and effective day surgery.
Fitness for a procedure should relate to the patient's functional status rather than ASA physical status.
It is possible to undertake most surgery in adults and children as day cases.
All day surgery units should have a clinical lead whose responsibilities include the development of local policies, guidelines and clinical governance.

All anaesthetists should be familiar with techniques that permit the patient to undergo a procedure with minimum stress and maximum comfort in order to enable early discharge, including regional nerve blocks and neuraxial blockade, such as spinal anaesthesia.
All members of the multidisciplinary team should be trained in day surgery practice.
High-quality, age-appropriate advice leaflets, assessment forms and protocols for specific procedures should be in place.
Day surgery should take place within a dedicated unit or area within the main hospital site.
Quality assurance and improvement programmes are an essential component of good care in all aspects of day surgery.

clinical.summaryText: These guidelines, published by the Association of Anaesthetists and the British Association of Day Surgery (BADS), outline the organizational and clinical management of anaesthesia for day-case surgery in adults and children. The guidance emphasizes that day surgery should be the default option for most elective surgical procedures, focusing on patient functional status rather than ASA physical status to determine fitness. Key components of a successful day surgery service include thorough pre-anaesthetic assessment, protocol-driven discharge, dedicated day surgery units, and a multidisciplinary team trained in day surgery practice. Anaesthetists are encouraged to utilize techniques that minimize stress and maximize comfort, such as regional nerve blocks and neuraxial blockade, to facilitate early discharge.

clinical.title: Safe provision of anaesthesia in magnetic resonance units

steps.text: All hospitals providing a service for anaesthesia within the MR unit should have a lead anaesthetist responsible for provision of anaesthesia for MRI.

Training should be provided for all grades of anaesthetist delivering anaesthesia in this remote area; all anaesthetists should have an understanding of the hazards involved in anaesthetising a patient in the MRI unit.

Anaesthesia/sedation for a patient needing an MRI scan, including intensive care unit (ICU) patients, should take into account the patient’s pathophysiological status and the remote location of the MRI unit.
Whenever possible, anaesthesia in remote sites should be provided by appropriately experienced consultants.
When care is delegated to a trainee or Specialty and Associate Specialist (SAS) doctor, they should have the appropriate competencies and level of training.
It is not acceptable for inexperienced staff, unfamiliar with the MR environment, to manage a patient in this environment, particularly out-of-hours.

Patients must be accompanied to the scanner by appropriately trained staff members, and if an anaesthetic machine is being used, then the anaesthetist should be supported throughout by an anaesthetic assistant who should be suitably skilled, trained and familiar with the anaesthetic requirements.

All patients and staff must be screened for the presence of implants and devices that may be a contraindication to a safe scan. The referring team should discuss the safety of the devices with the MR Responsible Person and the anaesthetist to plan a suitable management strategy.
Anyone remaining in the scanning room should be provided with ear protection during scanning.
The MRI for patients should only be undertaken if the diagnostic benefit outweighs the risk. This discussion must involve the multidisciplinary team, particularly for a patient on the ICU.
The MR safety checklists for general anaesthesia, intra-operative MRI and for transfer of ICU patients should be used in conjunction with the World Health Organization (WHO) checklist.

clinical.summaryText:
These guidelines from the Association of Anaesthetists and the Neuro Anaesthesia and Critical Care Society of Great Britain and Ireland address the safe provision of anaesthesia in magnetic resonance (MR) units. With an increasing number of MR scanners, complex interventions, and MR-conditional implants, the guidelines emphasize the importance of multidisciplinary decision-making, appropriate staffing, and specialized training to manage the unique hazards of the MR environment.

Key recommendations focus on service organization, including appointing a lead anaesthetist, ensuring staff competency, and utilizing standardized safety checklists (such as the WHO checklist) alongside MR-specific screening. The guidelines highlight that anaesthesia in these remote, high-hazard environments should ideally be delivered by experienced consultants or appropriately trained staff with dedicated assistance.

clinical.title: The ‘NAP5 Handbook’: Concise practice guidance on the prevention and management of accidental awareness during general anaesthesia

steps.text: Inform all patients of the risks of general anaesthesia, including the possibility of accidental awareness during general anaesthesia (AAGA), before their surgery.
When consenting patients, use a form of words that proportionately conveys the risks of AAGA.
Emphasise during consent for sedation that the patient will be awake and therefore may have recall for at least parts of the procedure.
Identify patient or situational factors constituting a higher risk for AAGA (including organisational factors like overbooked or reorganised lists) and highlight these at the WHO premeet/team brief.

During induction of anaesthesia, adhere to suitable dosing of intravenous agent, check anaesthetic effect before paralysis or instrumentation of the airway, and maintain anaesthetic administration, including during transfer (facilitated by a simple ABCDE checklist).
If AAGA is suspected during maintenance (e.g., by patient movement), pay prompt attention to giving verbal reassurance to the patient, increasing analgesia, and deepening the level of anaesthesia.

For cases requiring paralysis, use the minimum dose of neuromuscular blocking drugs (NMBDs) that achieves sufficient neuromuscular blockade for surgery, and use a nerve stimulator as an essential monitor to titrate the dosing of NMBDs to this minimum.
Where total intravenous anaesthesia (TIVA) is used, adhere strictly to the relevant recently published guidelines.

At emergence, first confirm that surgery is complete, then ensure NMBDs are adequately reversed before allowing the patient to regain consciousness. Manage the patient experience, particularly during awake extubation, by speaking to the patient.
Manage any cases of suspected or confirmed AAGA using the NAP5 pathway as a guide.

clinical.summaryText: The NAP5 Handbook provides concise, practical guidance distilled from the 5th National Audit Project (NAP5) on preventing and managing accidental awareness during general anaesthesia (AAGA). It translates academic findings into actionable clinical steps, emphasizing patient consent, risk identification at the WHO team brief, structured induction and maintenance protocols, careful management of neuromuscular blocking drugs (NMBDs), and structured emergence and post-AAGA care.

clinical.title: Safe Drug Management in Anaesthetic Practice

steps.text: Develop and implement local Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for the safe storage and management of drugs used in anaesthetic practice. These SOPs must support the hierarchy of priorities (patient safety, healthcare professional safety, and public safety), be risk-assessed by a multidisciplinary group (pharmacists, anaesthetists, nurses, ODPs, clinical managers), and be ratified by the medicines management committee and endorsed by the Accountable Officer for Medicines.

Identify Immediately Available Drugs (IADs) that must be immediately accessible during clinical practice (e.g., neuromuscular blocking drugs and vasoconstrictors). IADs should accompany the anaesthetist and patient in an unlocked container (no keys, codes, or RFID required), though a tamper-evident sealed container requiring no tools to open is acceptable.

For clinical areas with unoccupied rooms requiring IADs (e.g., ED resuscitation rooms or Labour Ward theatres), store these drugs in an immediately accessible, unlocked container (e.g., a tamper-evident container in a closed but unlocked cupboard) as determined by local SOPs.

Develop SOPs for the storage, management, reconciliation, and disposal of drugs of diversion (e.g., propofol, potassium, neuromuscular blocking drugs, and midazolam). Consider treating non-Schedule 2 drugs of diversion with stricter record-keeping and requiring witnessed, recorded disposal by a second healthcare practitioner.

Foster a vigilant, open, supportive, and caring departmental culture that encourages awareness of diversion risks, helps identify and manage staff members at risk, and empowers all staff to speak up regarding unusual drug access or usage.

Acknowledge that while physical barriers and reconciliation protocols cannot fully prevent determined theft or diversion, limiting ready access acts as a vital barrier during times of extreme stress and sudden self-destructive impulses.

Strongly prioritize the use of manufacturer-prepared or pharmacy-prepared prefilled syringes. Prefilled syringes are advantageous as they are inherently tamper-evident and eliminate the need for additional complex tamper-evident storage containers.

Conduct regular multidisciplinary reviews and audits of drug storage SOPs, rapid access ease, and incident reports. Share audit findings with all drug administration staff and revise SOPs when necessary.
Implement effective access control systems for all entry routes to areas where anaesthesia is administered, limiting access strictly to personnel with legitimate clinical reasons.

clinical.summaryText:
This guideline, jointly produced by the Association of Anaesthetists and the Royal College of Anaesthetists (RCoA), addresses the safe management, storage, and reconciliation of drugs used in anaesthetic practice across various hospital locations (including operating theatres, ED, and Labour Wards). It establishes a clear hierarchy of priorities: patient safety first (ensuring rapid access to emergency drugs), followed by the safety of healthcare professionals and the public. It emphasizes the development of local Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) to balance immediate drug availability with security measures against drug diversion.

Key recommendations include identifying Immediately Available Drugs (IADs) that must remain unlocked and rapidly accessible, implementing robust SOPs for drugs of diversion (such as propofol and midazolam), promoting a supportive departmental culture to address healthcare worker wellbeing and diversion risks, and utilizing prefilled syringes to enhance safety and efficiency.

clinical.title: Ergonomics in the anaesthetic workplace

steps.text: Musculoskeletal injuries are common in anaesthetists and they should aim to make their posture as natural and neutral as possible at all times.
Wherever possible, patients should position themselves on the operating trolley or table and remain there for their procedure and first-stage recovery.

All purchased equipment should be assessed using ergonomic principles to ensure it is acceptable for its intended use and that it is adjustable to maintain comfort for the anaesthetist, including those who are pregnant or have disabilities.
All staff should receive regular teaching in manual handling as part of their mandatory training.

Room temperature should be at least 21 °C, the relative humidity should be in the range of 40–70%, there should be adequate and adjustable lighting and noise should be no louder than 80 dB and for short periods only.
Good ergonomic practice is one where the patient is at a height that approximates to the level of the umbilicus, T10 dermatome or waist of the anaesthetist.

If the anaesthetised patient is lying on an operating table or trolley for tracheal intubation, the table height should be adjusted until the patient’s forehead is at the level of the anaesthetist’s xiphoid process or nipple.

Tasks such as regional anaesthetic nerve blocks, central venous cannulation and siting of arterial lines should be performed with the preparation trolley on the side of the dominant hand, the ultrasound machine directly opposite the operator’s view and the monitor at eye level.

clinical.summaryText:
Ergonomics in relation to anaesthesia is the scientific study of the interaction between anaesthetists and their workspace environment in order to promote safety, performance, and well-being. Musculoskeletal problems, such as back pain, account for a significant portion of sickness absence among healthcare staff. To avoid pain or discomfort, anaesthetists must adopt and maintain a natural, neutral posture, keeping arms close to the body and avoiding reaching or looking behind their sitting or standing position.

This guideline outlines key recommendations for optimizing the physical environment, including maintaining room temperatures of at least 21°C, relative humidity of 40-70%, and noise levels below 80 dB. It provides specific postural and height adjustments for clinical tasks, such as positioning the operating table to align the patient's forehead with the anaesthetist's xiphoid process during intubation, and placing equipment like ultrasound monitors directly opposite the operator's view at eye level.

clinical.title: National Essential Anaesthesia Drugs List (NEADL)

steps.text: Encourage the use of alternatives to essential drugs to reduce pressure on supply and spread familiarity with substitutes. When shortages occur, prioritize finding direct injectable alternatives (e.g., lorazepam for midazolam or vecuronium for rocuronium).

When injectable drug shortages persist, develop protocols utilizing non-injectable formulations or alternative methods (e.g., using oral analgesics and long-acting opioids instead of injectable opioids for orthopaedic surgery).

Implement structured mitigation strategies during drug shortages: Decrease waste by only drawing up necessary amounts and considering alternatives to patient-controlled analgesia (PCA). Decrease use by developing alternative techniques (e.g., regional analgesia instead of opioids). Decrease supplies by providing clinicians with the best available alternatives and safe usage advice. Decrease activity by triaging care to protect emergency and life-saving procedures.
Ensure early communication between relevant bodies (DHSC, MHRA) and anaesthesia/critical care professionals to facilitate timely information exchange regarding drug shortages.

clinical.summaryText: The NEADL provides a minimum set of essential drugs required for safe anaesthesia delivery. The British National Formulary online (BNF online) offers the most up-to-date details of drugs available for anaesthesia. While the list is not prescriptive for anaesthetists, it emphasizes that alternatives should be encouraged to maintain supply sustainability and reduce the likelihood of shortages. Shortage management requires a structured approach involving looking beyond injectables, implementing mitigation strategies (such as decreasing waste, use, supplies, or activity), and maintaining robust communication across all healthcare sectors.

clinical.title: Recommendations for standards of monitoring during anaesthesia and recovery 2021

steps.text: Adequate supervision requires that an anaesthetist should be present throughout the conduct of anaesthesia or the administration of procedural sedation (In hospitals employing Anaesthesia Associates [AAs] in the UK, this responsibility may be delegated to an AA, supervised by a consultant anaesthetist).

General anaesthesia requires minimum monitoring of ECG, SpO2, NIBP and capnography, which should be checked for correct function and begun before induction of anaesthesia and continue throughout anaesthesia, transfer to the post-anaesthesia care unit (PACU) and recovery. Age-adjusted minimum alveolar concentration (MAC) should be monitored during use of inhaled anaesthetic drugs. Capnography should be continued until any artificial airway is removed and a response to verbal contact re-established.
Regional anaesthesia requires minimum monitoring of ECG, NIBP and SpO2 which should begin before the procedure, and should be continued for at least 30 min after block completion.
Procedural sedation requires minimum monitoring of ECG, SpO2 and NIBP. Capnography should be used during procedural sedation whenever there is loss of response to verbal contact.

Transfer requires minimum monitoring of ECG, SpO2 and NIBP. If an airway device remains in place capnography should be used during the transfer of anaesthetised or sedated patients, including from the operating theatre to the PACU.

Quantitative neuromuscular monitoring should be used whenever neuromuscular blocking (NMB) drugs are administered, throughout all phases of anaesthesia from before initiation of neuromuscular blockade until recovery of the train-of-four ratio to > 0.9 has been confirmed.

Processed electroencephalogram (pEEG) monitoring should be used when total intravenous anaesthesia (TIVA) is administered together with a NMB drug. It should start before induction and continue at least until full recovery from the effects of the neuromuscular blockade has been confirmed. It should be considered during other anaesthetic techniques including inhalational anaesthesia and for the high-risk patient.

Capillary blood glucose and ketone monitoring should be immediately accessible in every location where patients are anaesthetised and blood glucose should be measured at least hourly in patients with treated diabetes.
Alarm limits for all equipment should be set to patient-specific values before use. Audible alarms should be enabled during anaesthesia.

An anaesthetic record should be made with an accurate summary of information provided by all monitoring devices. We recommend automated electronic anaesthetic record systems and that these be integrated into the hospital’s electronic health record system.

Additional equipment and monitoring that anaesthetists should have access to should include blood gas analysis and haemoglobin measurement and flexible bronchoscopy (for confirmation of tube placement in the airway).

clinical.summaryText: This guideline updates and replaces the 5th edition of the Standards of Monitoring published in 2015. The aim of this document is to provide guidance on the minimum standards for monitoring of any patient undergoing anaesthesia or sedation under the care of an anaesthetist. Key recommendations include mandatory minimum monitoring (ECG, SpO2, NIBP, and capnography) for general anaesthesia, regional anaesthesia, and procedural sedation, alongside updated indications for quantitative neuromuscular monitoring and processed electroencephalogram (pEEG) monitoring.

clinical.title: Syringe labelling in anaesthesia and critical care areas: review 2022

steps.text: Ensure all syringes containing drugs are labelled. Always check the drug ampoule and correctly label the syringe with the correct label. Do not use blank coloured labels as they are a potential source of confusion.
For drugs of opposite action (and antagonists), ensure the diagonal stripes on each side of the drug name are at least 15% of the label length.
For muscle relaxant reversal drug labels, ensure a transverse black line is present beneath the drug name to distinguish them from other drugs of opposite action.

Use white lettering on the labels for adrenaline, suxamethonium, and benzodiazepines to improve prominence and prevent confusion (such as confusing the orange of benzodiazepines with the warm red of muscle relaxants).
Remove the black border for the protamine label, but retain it for the heparin label.

Position the drug concentration clearly: below the drug name to the right for most labels. For drugs of opposite action, ensure it is not printed over diagonal lines but below the drug name and aligned with the right-hand edge. For pre-printed concentrations, central positioning below the drug name is preferred.

For premixed drugs in a single ampoule (e.g., neostigmine and glycopyrronium), use a background colour that reflects the intended action of the drug mixture (e.g., muscle relaxant reversal), with the secondary drug printed below the primary drug.
Utilise Tall Man lettering where appropriate to differentiate certain specified drugs and prevent look-alike, sound-alike errors.

clinical.summaryText: This guideline provides updated recommendations for user-applied syringe labelling in anaesthesia and critical care areas, aligning with BS ISO 26825:2020. It details critical design modifications aimed at reducing drug administration errors, including precise specifications for diagonal stripes on antagonist labels, transverse black lines for muscle relaxant reversals, and specific lettering color changes (white lettering) for high-alert drugs like adrenaline, suxamethonium, and benzodiazepines. Additionally, it emphasizes that all syringes must be labelled, blank coloured labels must be avoided, and drug concentrations must be clearly positioned. For premixed drugs, the label background must reflect the primary intended clinical action.

clinical.title: Suicide amongst anaesthetists 2019

steps.text: The recording and/or reporting of suicides as a cause of death should be improved. In particular, where the deceased is a doctor there should be an improved mechanism for identifying and recording his/her specialty.
All anaesthetists have a part to play in supporting the mental well-being of colleagues and contributing to the creation and maintenance of a mentally healthy workplace.

All anaesthetic departments should identify an individual(s) – who may not be an anaesthetist – with a lead role in supporting the mental health of at-risk staff. This person should act as a focal point for information and signposting and, where necessary, liaise with other relevant staff (e.g. occupational physicians, Trust well-being leads, human resource departments, clinical directors, College Tutors, etc.) and keep abreast of national and local initiatives regarding mental well-being.

There should be ongoing education within departments and organisations about suicide and safer ways to intervene. Such educational activity should include efforts to counter the stigma associated with mental ill health, and to support colleagues who may be struggling, for example, with financial, relationship or professional stresses including complaints and/or disciplinary procedures.

Specialist medical input should be involved early, with the anaesthetist’s consent; this may include his/her general practitioner (GP), psychiatrist, occupational physician or external agencies such as the Practitioner Health Programme. The confidentiality of individuals must be respected at all times.
Individuals at particular risk, or in distress, should be encouraged to draw up a ‘safety plan’. All staff should be registered (or encouraged to register) with a GP.
All departments should have a plan for managing staff-related crises including death by suicide.

clinical.summaryText:
Anaesthetists are recognized as having an increased risk of suicide compared to other medical specialties and the general population. Key risk factors include access to and knowledge of lethal drugs (with poisoning, particularly using anaesthetic medications like propofol, accounting for the vast majority of cases), high physical and mental demands, occupational fatigue, perfectionist personality traits, and a reluctance to seek help due to stigma or confidentiality concerns.

These guidelines aim to raise awareness, reduce stigma, and provide actionable recommendations for departments and individuals. Key recommendations include improving the reporting of suicides by specialty, establishing departmental mental health leads, providing ongoing education on suicide intervention, involving specialist medical input early with consent, encouraging the creation of personal safety plans, and ensuring all departments have a crisis management plan in place.

clinical.title: Better musculoskeletal health for anaesthetists

steps.text: Pay close attention to both physical and psychological risk factors that may lead to the development or exacerbation of musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs).
Perform ongoing risk assessments and strictly adhere to published standards of health and safety at work, including relevant training.
Adopt a multidisciplinary approach involving employers, anaesthetists, equipment designers, and occupational medicine departments to prevent and manage MSDs.
Identify and minimise causal or aggravating work-related risk factors, including mechanical stressors (e.g., heavy lifting, awkward postures) and psychosocial factors.
Evaluate the impact of MSDs on employees' ability to work effectively and provide appropriate occupational health support.

Optimise the ergonomics of the anaesthetic workplace, focusing on workstation/anaesthetic machine design, patient transfer mechanisms, and posture during clinical procedures like tracheal intubation, nerve blocks, and central venous cannulation.

clinical.summaryText:
Work-related musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs) are highly prevalent among healthcare workers, with evidence indicating that anaesthetists are at a higher risk of upper limb disorders compared to other groups. This consensus guideline, developed by the Association of Anaesthetists and the Society of Occupational Medicine, aims to compile advice and recommendations to reduce the prevalence and severity of MSDs and prevent the exacerbation of pre-existing conditions.

Key risk factors in anaesthetic practice include years of practice, parenthood, right-handedness (potentially due to traditional laryngoscopy posture), manual patient transfers, and poorly designed heavy workstations. Addressing these risks requires a multidisciplinary approach, continuous risk assessments, adherence to health and safety standards, and ergonomic improvements in the workplace.

clinical.title: Substance use disorder in the anaesthetist

steps.text: Treat substance use disorder (alcohol or drugs) in an anaesthetist as an illness rather than criminal behaviour, ensuring the doctor is afforded the same confidentiality as any other patient.
Appoint a named clinician as the lead for well-being in every department of anaesthesia (this individual does not necessarily have to be an anaesthetist).
Educate all anaesthetic and critical care staff on the early signs and symptoms of substance use disorder to facilitate early recognition, which is key to successful treatment and return to work.
Defer local disciplinary hearings or fitness to practice hearings while the doctor is actively undergoing treatment and rehabilitation.

Strengthen security and accountability around potential drugs of diversion. Develop local standard operating procedures (SOPs) for the storage, management, and safe disposal of opioids, midazolam, propofol, and other anaesthetic drugs.
Ensure uniform access to treatment programmes for anaesthetists with substance use disorder throughout the UK and Ireland.

Involve the partner or family of the anaesthetist in the care team early in the treatment process, obtaining consent early, as their support is crucial for treatment and identifying potential relapse behaviours.
Consider advising a career transition to a specialty other than anaesthesia for individuals with propofol or inhalational agent use disorder, particularly if the affected doctor is still a trainee.

clinical.summaryText:
Anaesthetists experience a higher incidence of substance use disorder compared to other medical specialties, largely due to easy access to highly addictive intravenous opioids, propofol, midazolam, and inhalational agents. Alcohol use disorder remains the most common presentation. Early recognition of warning signs—such as mood swings, withdrawal, increased work hours, and unusual drug-signing patterns—is critical, as the first presentation of the disorder can unfortunately be an accidental or deliberate overdose. With appropriate treatment, support, and supervision, over 75% of affected anaesthetists successfully return to full clinical practice.

This guideline emphasizes treating substance use disorder as an illness rather than criminal behavior, ensuring patient confidentiality, and establishing a designated well-being lead in every department. It recommends strengthening local standard operating procedures for drug security, deferring disciplinary hearings during active rehabilitation, involving family support early, and considering alternative specialties for those with propofol or inhalational agent addictions.

clinical.title: Catastrophe Handbook

steps.text: Understand the scope and definitions of a catastrophe (Section 1).
Implement personal and organizational preparation strategies before an incident occurs (Section 2).
Execute immediate actions based on the nature of the catastrophe: clinical involvement, non-clinical roles, or leadership responsibilities (Section 3).
Manage communication through structured team meetings and departmental updates (Section 4).
Address psychological responses for affected individuals and manage trauma (Section 5).
Ensure comprehensive support and recovery planning, including actions for leaders to maintain service continuity (Section 6).
Manage special situations such as resident doctor issues, professional investigations, or civil litigation (Section 7).

clinical.summaryText:
This handbook provides a comprehensive guide for managing major incidents and catastrophes within the healthcare setting. It outlines structured protocols covering preparation, immediate response actions, communication strategies, and psychological support throughout the crisis lifecycle. Key areas include defining roles—whether clinical or non-clinical—and adapting responses based on direct involvement versus leadership positions. Furthermore, it emphasizes the critical importance of post-incident care, focusing on professional support, individual recovery, and maintaining service continuity for both staff and patients.

The guide also addresses complex special situations, such as managing investigations (GMC/Coroner's) or dealing with legal fallout. By providing structured tools like SBAR and checklists, it ensures a systematic approach to communication and care during high-stress events.

clinical.title: Peri-operative pain management in adults: a multidisciplinary consensus statement from the Association of Anaesthetists and the British Pain Society

steps.text: Every hospital should have a pain team to help ensure compliance with optimal peri-operative pain management, to educate staff, to write and implement local guidelines, and to ensure appropriate patient information documentation is available.
Throughout the peri-operative pathway, pain assessment should be used to guide analgesic management and should rely on measures of function, rather than unidimensional pain scores.

Throughout the peri-operative pathway and, where not contraindicated, multimodal analgesia should be encouraged, including paracetamol; non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs; alpha-2 adrenergic agonists; N-methyl-D-aspartate antagonists; corticosteroids; and regional analgesia.

Patients should be screened pre-operatively for modifiable risk factors for severe acute pain and chronic postsurgical pain. This includes the use of high doses of opioids; frailty; anxiety; depression; and lifestyle factors (weight, smoking, alcohol and drug use).

Pre-operative shared decision-making and expectation management should include a discussion of the risks and benefits of peri-operative pain management strategies, including regional anaesthetic techniques.
Analgesia should be multimodal and be of sufficient duration to extend analgesia to the postoperative period.
Intra-operative pain management should take a personalised medicine approach and use evidence-based procedure-specific recommendations.
When analgesics, including opioids, are used, the lowest effective dose and oral routes should be used when possible, and initial doses should be age-appropriate.
Modified-release formulations and compound opioid preparations should not be used routinely for acute postoperative pain.

There should be local protocols for the prescription of discharge medicines after surgery, of no greater than 7 days duration, with guidance for patients on how to seek support for any ongoing pain management needs after 7 days.

clinical.summaryText:
Nearly half of adult patients undergoing surgery experience moderate or severe postoperative pain, which can hamper recovery and lead to adverse outcomes. This multidisciplinary consensus statement from the Association of Anaesthetists and the British Pain Society provides key principles to optimize peri-operative pain management across the entire surgical pathway, from pre-admission to post-discharge.

The guidelines emphasize the transition from unidimensional pain scores to functional pain assessments, the routine use of multimodal analgesia (including regional techniques), pre-operative screening for modifiable risk factors, and shared decision-making. Additionally, they advocate for safe opioid stewardship, recommending the lowest effective dose, avoiding routine modified-release or compound opioids, and limiting discharge prescriptions to a maximum of 7 days with clear follow-up plans.

clinical.title: Elective peri-operative management of adults taking glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists, glucose-dependent insulinotropic peptide agonists and sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitors

steps.text: Continue glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1RAs) and dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic peptide (GIP)/GLP-1 receptor agonists before elective surgery.
Perform a comprehensive peri-operative risk assessment and stratification for delayed gastric emptying, regurgitation, and pulmonary aspiration.
Implement peri-operative techniques and precautions to mitigate the risk of pulmonary aspiration before, during, and after sedation or general anaesthesia.
Omit sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 (SGLT2) inhibitors on the day before and the day of the elective procedure to reduce the risk of euglycaemic diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA).
Engage in a shared decision-making approach with all patients, discussing specific risks and mitigation strategies associated with these medications.

clinical.summaryText: This multidisciplinary consensus statement provides pragmatic, expert-backed guidance for the elective peri-operative management of adults taking GLP-1 receptor agonists, dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonists, and SGLT2 inhibitors. Given the increasing use of these agents for diabetes mellitus, obesity, heart failure, and chronic kidney disease, standardized management is essential to mitigate serious peri-operative risks. The guidelines recommend continuing GLP-1/GIP agonists with strict aspiration risk mitigation strategies, while SGLT2 inhibitors must be omitted the day before and the day of surgery to prevent euglycaemic diabetic ketoacidosis.

clinical.title: Peri-operative identification and management of patients with unhealthy alcohol intake

steps.text: Screen patients during pre-assessment for surgery using an appropriate tool, such as the Alcohol Use Disorders Identification Test Consumption (AUDIT) questionnaire, to triage risk relating to alcohol use.

Perform a careful pre-operative physical examination and additional relevant investigations for patients with harmful chronic alcohol intake (suggested by an AUDIT score > 19 and/or consumption of > 35 units/week for women or > 50 units/week for men) or those at high risk of alcohol-related liver disease.
Initiate a proportional response to elevated AUDIT questionnaire scores, ranging from brief intervention to inpatient specialist referral.
Maintain clinical suspicion and recognize that patients with a history of harmful alcohol consumption remain at high risk of alcohol-related liver disease, irrespective of current screening scores.
Guide risk assessment and decision-making in patients with established alcohol-related liver disease using validated scoring systems such as the Surgical Outcome Risk Tool (SORT).

Consider surgical urgency according to recognized classification systems. Delay elective procedures if appropriate to allow risk-reduction interventions, and ensure emergency surgical populations are screened and managed to mitigate alcohol-related risks.

Assess suitability for day-case surgery by carefully investigating and discussing potential barriers, including organ dysfunction, social circumstances, and concerns regarding immediate post-discharge alcohol consumption.

Employ analgesic techniques that reduce opioid requirements, including regional methods. Modify or avoid common analgesics and seek specialist acute pain service assistance for patients with cirrhosis and alcoholic hepatitis who are at elevated risk of opioid-related complications.
Actively monitor patients with harmful alcohol intake for postoperative complications such as infections, arrhythmias, bleeding, and delirium, with due consideration for critical care admission.

Perform regular peri-operative assessments for alcohol withdrawal using objective scoring systems like the Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment Alcohol - Revised (CIWA-Ar) scale. Use short-acting benzodiazepines to manage withdrawal in older patients or those with underlying liver cirrhosis to avoid drug-related complications.

clinical.summaryText:
This consensus guideline from the Association of Anaesthetists provides practical, evidence-based recommendations for the peri-operative identification and management of patients with unhealthy alcohol intake undergoing elective and emergency surgery. The guideline emphasizes systematic pre-operative screening using validated tools like the AUDIT questionnaire, risk stratification using scoring systems such as SORT, and tailored clinical pathways to mitigate the high risk of postoperative complications, organ dysfunction, and acute alcohol withdrawal.

Key recommendations focus on proportional interventions, careful assessment of day-case suitability, opioid-sparing analgesic strategies, and structured monitoring for withdrawal using the CIWA-Ar scale. Special precautions are highlighted for patients with established liver cirrhosis or advanced age, particularly regarding the choice of short-acting benzodiazepines to prevent drug-related complications.

clinical.title: Association of Anaesthetists guidelines: safe vascular access 2025

steps.text: Hospitals should establish dedicated vascular access specialist teams with a designated clinical lead. Anaesthetists are in a strong position to lead on this multidisciplinary approach to the provision and development of vascular access services.

Vascular access specialist teams should work with other stakeholders to ensure appropriate hospital and community vascular access standards and to monitor these standards against locally agreed key performance indicators, using regular audit and review. Responsiveness indicators should be included to ensure timely and expert insertion.

A comprehensive and holistic approach to vascular access for all patients is paramount. This entails thorough vein and lifestyle assessments; tailored discussions and consent procedures; safe insertion practices; meticulous documentation; early identification and management of complications; and timely removal of devices, all aimed at preserving long-term vein health.

Every patient should have timely access to the insertion of commonly used vascular access devices. For patients classified as having difficult intravenous access, consideration should be given to early involvement of specialised vascular access teams for long-term access solutions.

The integration of Local Safety Standards for Invasive Procedures (LocSSIPs) into vascular access procedures is essential, including mechanisms to prevent retained guidewires and minimise complications. Steps should include: appropriate consent and procedural verification; team brief; sign-in; time-out; safe insertion with reconciliation of equipment; sign-out; and handover, debrief and documentation. Lines or dilators that have been placed in an artery inadvertently and are >= 6 Fr should be left in place and their safe removal discussed urgently with interventional radiologists or vascular surgeons.

Real-time ultrasound guidance should be employed for all central vein access and other applicable access sites to enhance the success and safety of procedures; a 'scouting' ultrasound should take place immediately before the procedure using sterile ultrasound gel. When ultrasound is necessary for peripheral cannulation, consideration should be given to using a long peripheral catheter or midline over a short peripheral catheter to mitigate the risk of extravasation injury.

To preserve vessel health, catheters should ideally occupy no more than one-third of the cross-sectional area of the vessel, with at least one-third of the catheter situated intraluminally. Selecting the appropriate vein to maintain a haemodilution ratio of >= 3 (three parts blood to one part drug) helps minimise vein wall damage from irritant agents. Larger, high-flow veins provide better dilution and are preferred for such drugs.

Optimal positioning for upper body central venous catheter tips includes the lower third of the superior vena cava, cavo-atrial junction or high right atrium; this ensures maximum blood flow and dilution and reduces the risk of vessel injury and thrombosis. The movement of peripherally inserted central catheters into the body with arm adduction should be considered. Real-time imaging or ECG guidance should be used for long-term catheters. Lower body catheter tips should ideally be sited in the inferior vena cava, with long-term catheter tips positioned above the level of the renal veins.

Recommendations are given for the management of patients undergoing anticoagulation therapy or those with underlying coagulation disorders. Local protocols should be developed, aligning with national guidance. Different sizes and types of vascular access device require different safety thresholds. In general, no treatment is required before an uncomplicated insertion of a peripherally inserted central catheter or non-tunnelled central venous catheter.

The presence of a central venous catheter is a major predisposing factor to venous thrombosis. Regarding catheter-related central venous thrombosis, if the catheter remains necessary, in good position, uninfected, functional and not jeopardising the limb, it may be left in situ, with the patient receiving anticoagulation as per local protocols.

In patients with chronic kidney disease who may require renal replacement therapy, consideration of vein preservation and long-term vein health is vital. Protection of veins that may be required for the creation of an arteriovenous fistula (such as the basilic and cephalic vein) is particularly important.

Mastectomy and axillary lymph node resection are not considered absolute contraindications for short-term ipsilateral vein cannulation. Peripherally inserted central catheter insertion and axillary/subclavian vein insertion should be avoided in arms at risk or with evidence of lymphoedema.

Potent peripheral vasoconstrictors such as noradrenaline may be infused for short durations through appropriately positioned short peripheral catheters and at dilute concentrations. Strict protocols should be followed.
Training in postinsertion care, maintenance, flushing techniques and early complication recognition should be mandatory for all healthcare providers who work with vascular access devices.
Training schemes in advanced vascular access should be developed at a national level; where appropriate, these should be open to non-medical staff.

clinical.summaryText: Safe vascular access is integral to anaesthetic and critical care practice. Despite technological and procedural advances, it remains a frequent source of adverse events and patient harm. These guidelines from the Association of Anaesthetists (AAGBI) provide a comprehensive, consensus-based framework for the selection, insertion, and care of vascular access devices, emphasizing a holistic approach to long-term vessel health. Key recommendations cover the establishment of dedicated vascular access specialist teams, the integration of Local Safety Standards for Invasive Procedures (LocSSIPs), real-time ultrasound guidance, optimal catheter tip positioning, and specific management strategies for anticoagulated patients, renal replacement therapy candidates, and post-mastectomy patients.

clinical.title: The use of blood components and their alternatives 2024

steps.text: All patients should have their haemoglobin concentration measured before listing for major elective surgery.
Where blood transfusion is anticipated, this and alternatives to transfusion should be discussed with the patient before surgery, and their consent should be documented according to local protocols.
Management of anticoagulants in the peri-operative period should balance the risk of bleeding with the risk of thrombosis.

The use of cell salvage and antifibrinolytics, such as tranexamic acid, is recommended in all patients who are bleeding, where blood loss > 500 ml (> 8 ml.kg -1 in children weighing > 10 kg) is possible and/or in patients are unable to receive donor blood.
Minimise iatrogenic anaemia by managing blood sampling appropriately.
Early recognition of obstetric bleeding is essential and should be measured accumulatively, leading to a clear escalation plan of intervention and involvement of the multidisciplinary team.

Every institution should have a major haemorrhage protocol which is regularly audited and reviewed. They should be concise but targeted, and allow for immediate release and protocolised administration of blood components.
In an emergency, surgical implementation of damage/proximal control strategies may be required until haemorrhage control is achieved.

Group O red blood cells (RBCs) for transfusion should be readily available in the clinical area, in case haemorrhage is life-threatening. Group-specific RBCs should be made available by the laboratory as soon as possible after receiving correctly labelled samples and being informed of the emergency requirement for blood, and according to local laboratory protocols.

During major haemorrhage due to trauma, preference should be given to transfusing RBCs and fresh frozen plasma (FFP). Clear fluids (crystalloids) should be avoided unless there is profound hypotension and no imminent availability of blood components.
Blood components should be prescribed for children as a volume (ml.kg -1) rather than in units.

Patients who continue to bleed actively should be monitored by point-of-care and/or regular laboratory tests for coagulation, fibrinogen and platelet counts and/or function. A guide for transfusion should be: FFP if INR > 1.5; cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen < 1.5 g.l-1 (< 2.0 g.l-1 in obstetrics); and platelets if platelet count < 50 × 10^9.l-1 (< 75 × 10^9.l-1 in obstetrics).

clinical.summaryText:
These guidelines from the Association of Anaesthetists provide evidence-based recommendations for the optimal use of blood components and their alternatives in peri-operative care. Driven by findings from large randomised controlled trials and improved haemovigilance, the guidelines emphasize patient blood management (PBM) principles, including pre-operative anaemia detection, minimising blood loss, and optimizing physiological reserve. Key recommendations focus on organizational readiness, targeted major haemorrhage protocols, and specific transfusion thresholds for active bleeding.

Additionally, the guidelines highlight strategies to avoid unnecessary transfusions, manage anticoagulant therapy, and minimize iatrogenic anaemia. Special considerations are outlined for obstetric, paediatric, and trauma patients, advocating for a multidisciplinary approach involving anaesthetists, intensivists, surgeons, obstetricians, and haematologists to ensure safe, timely, and targeted transfusion support.

clinical.title: Measurement and management of adult blood pressure in the peri-operative period

steps.text: Patients referred for elective surgery should have a blood pressure measurement taken in a clinical setting in primary or secondary care in the past 12 months < 160/100 mmHg, or an ambulatory or home blood pressure measurement < 155/95 mmHg. Secondary care peri-operative teams should accept these blood pressure measurements.
If primary care blood pressure readings from the last 12 months are undocumented in the referral letter, these should be requested.

Patients who attend pre-operative assessment clinic without evidence of a clinic blood pressure measurement < 160/100 mmHg or an ambulatory or home blood pressure measurement < 155/95 mmHg being documented by primary or secondary care in the preceding 12 months should have their blood pressure measured.

Patients who attend the pre-operative assessment clinic without documentation of normotension in primary care may only proceed to elective surgery if their clinic blood pressure measurement is < 180/120 mmHg or ambulatory or home blood pressure measurement is < 175/115 mmHg.
Pre-operative assessment clinics should be utilized to identify adverse effects of antihypertensive therapy and pre-operative hypotension, and to address these before surgery.

Patients at risk of postural hypotension (including those who are symptomatic, aged > 80 years, or diabetic) should be screened with a supine-to-stand or sit-to-stand blood pressure assessment. If a significant drop is detected (drop of 20 mmHg systolic and/or 10 mmHg diastolic within 3 minutes), review medication, manage blood pressure to standing values, and consider specialist referral if it persists.
Patients should normally take their antihypertensive therapy, including angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors and angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs), on the day of surgery.

Avoid excess and prolonged intra-operative hypotension. Target intra-operative blood pressure to the individual patient and surgical procedure. Common targets in higher-risk patients are mean arterial pressure (MAP) > 70 mmHg and/or systolic blood pressure (SBP) > 100 mmHg.

In patients at greater risk of peri-operative blood pressure complications, reduce the interval of non-invasive blood pressure measurements and maintain a low threshold for continuous invasive blood pressure monitoring.
Manage postoperative hypotension in a timely manner using standard treatments, targeting the patient's pre-operative blood pressure, surgical procedure, and postoperative physiology.
Reintroduce antihypertensive therapy in the postoperative period according to the patient's blood pressure.

clinical.summaryText:
This guideline provides updated recommendations from the Association of Anaesthetists and the British and Irish Hypertension Society on the measurement and management of adult blood pressure in the peri-operative period. It outlines thresholds for proceeding with elective surgery, emphasizing that patients with documented clinic blood pressure < 160/100 mmHg or home/ambulatory blood pressure < 155/95 mmHg within the past 12 months can proceed. For patients without documented normotension, elective surgery may proceed if clinic blood pressure is < 180/120 mmHg or home/ambulatory blood pressure is < 175/115 mmHg.

Additionally, the guideline addresses the identification and management of postural hypotension, the continuation of antihypertensive medications (including ACE inhibitors and ARBs) on the day of surgery, and intra-operative targets (such as maintaining MAP > 70 mmHg and/or SBP > 100 mmHg in high-risk patients) to avoid prolonged hypotension.
